_note: Original Debug Part 2 questions. Difficulty rises through edge cases, nested state, and multi-step logic while staying introductory.

questions.title: Empty input divides by zero
questions.prompt: Which input exposes the bug, and what should be checked?
choices: An empty collection; handle zero items before dividing
A one-item collection; skip the first value
Negative values; convert them to strings
Duplicate values; sort them first
questions.explanation: The normal case hides the missing edge-case check. When there are no values, the divisor is zero. Decide what an empty average should mean before performing division.

questions.title: Row total never resets
questions.prompt: Why does the second row total include values from the first row?
choices: rowTotal is initialized outside the outer loop
The inner loop should run only once
best must start at 1
Rows must be processed backward
questions.explanation: State that belongs to one row must be reset for each row. Move rowTotal = 0 inside the outer loop before scanning the current row.

questions.title: Binary search discards the target
questions.prompt: What is wrong when the middle value is smaller than the target?
choices: The lower bound should move to mid + 1, not mid - 1
The upper bound should always increase
The list should be searched from both ends
mid must always be zero
questions.explanation: A middle value below the target means the target can only be to the right. Moving low backward discards the correct half and may repeat positions.

questions.title: Removing while moving forward
questions.prompt: Why can adjacent negative values be skipped?
choices: Removing an item shifts the next value into the current index
Negative values cannot be compared
The loop must start at index 1
The collection becomes read-only after removal
questions.explanation: After removal, later values shift left while the loop advances right. Iterate backward, adjust the index, or build a separate filtered collection.

questions.title: The best value fails on negatives
questions.prompt: Why can this report 0 when every value is negative?
choices: best starts at a value that may not exist in the data
The comparison should use ==
The loop should skip negative values
The collection must be sorted first
questions.explanation: Zero is not a safe initial maximum for all-negative input. Initialize best from the first actual value, then compare the remaining values.

python.code:
best = 0
for n in [-8, -2, -5]:
    if n > best: best = n
print(best)

questions.title: Duplicate maximum breaks second place
questions.prompt: Why might this report the same value for largest and second largest?
choices: It does not require the second value to be distinct from largest
It ignores values below zero
It begins at index 0
It uses two variables instead of a list
questions.explanation: Second largest usually means a distinct value. The update must reject a value equal to largest before considering it for second place.

questions.title: Prefix sum starts at the wrong place
questions.prompt: Why is the first output value already too large?
choices: total should start at 0, not at the first value before the loop
The loop must begin with the last value
The output collection should start with 1
Addition should be multiplication
questions.explanation: The loop adds the first value itself, so preloading total with that same value counts it twice. Start at zero or skip the first iteration deliberately.

python.code:
nums = [2, 3, 5]
total = nums[0]
running = []
for n in nums:
    total += n
    running.append(total)

questions.title: Palindrome compares the same side
questions.prompt: Why does this fail to compare mirrored characters?
choices: Both indexes point from the left; the second must count from the right
Strings cannot be indexed
The loop must inspect characters twice
The comparison should use assignment
questions.explanation: A palindrome check pairs position i with length - i - 1. Using i + 1 only compares neighboring characters on the same side.
